title: Why Your Shower Has Better Ideas Than Your Desk (A Neuroscience Investigation)
excerpt: Your brain's best work happens when you stop trying. Here's why, and what AI diffusion models accidentally proved about creativity.

title: The Fermi Paradox But For Good Software (Where Is Everybody?)
excerpt: If we have millions of developers and decades of practice, why does most software still suck?

title: Every New Year's Resolution Is a Negotiation With a Stranger
excerpt: On temporal discounting, personal identity, and why you're basically doing RLHF on yourself with a 365-day feedback loop.

title: The Paradox of Choice But For Netflix (Why You Spend 45 Minutes Choosing a 90-Minute Movie)
excerpt: More options should mean more satisfaction. Instead, they mean more paralysis, more regret, and more time staring at thumbnails.

title: The Internet Was Supposed to Make Us Smarter and I Have the Graph to Prove It Didn't
excerpt: All of human knowledge, in your pocket, used primarily to settle bar arguments and watch cats.

title: Why Autocomplete Knows You Better Than Your Therapist
excerpt: Your typing patterns, search history, and autocomplete suggestions form a more accurate psychological profile than most self-assessments.

title: The Dunning-Kruger Peak Is Where All the Confidence Lives
excerpt: The sweet spot where you know just enough to be dangerously wrong — and why AI models go through the exact same thing during training.

title: Your Phone's Battery Percentage Is a Metaphor for Everything
excerpt: How we manage phone battery maps perfectly onto how we manage energy, attention, motivation, and relationships.

title: The Turing Test Was Always the Wrong Test (And Alan Would Probably Agree)
excerpt: Why fooling a human into thinking a machine is human is a terrible benchmark for intelligence.

title: Why Every Meeting Could Have Been an Email (But Also Shouldn't Have Been)
excerpt: The bandwidth problem in communication — meetings, emails, and Slack as network protocols.

title: The Algorithm Doesn't Know You. It Knows a Puppet That Looks Like You.
excerpt: Recommendation algorithms don't model the real you — they model a simplified statistical puppet built from your clicks.

title: Sleep Is Just Your Brain's Garbage Collection Day
excerpt: Your brain literally pressure-washes itself every night. Dreams are just the test suite running edge cases.

title: The 10,000 Hour Rule Is a Lie (But the Truth Is Weirder)
excerpt: Malcolm Gladwell misquoted the research. The truth about expertise is weirder and more nuanced.

title: Why We're All Running Someone Else's Code
excerpt: Your beliefs, habits, and identity are programs you didn't write. So are an AI's.

title: Love Is Just Two Reward Functions That Accidentally Aligned (A Valentine's Day Special)
excerpt: Oxytocin, dopamine, and the alignment problem — in relationships and in AI.

title: The Trolley Problem Is Easy. The Real Problem Is the Pedestrian Detection.
excerpt: The actual hard problem in self-driving cars is way more boring and way more important than you think.

title: Why You Can't Remember Where You Put Your Keys But Can Quote Entire Movie Scenes
excerpt: Memory encoding isn't about importance — it's about emotional weight, narrative structure, and repetition.

title: The Map Is Not the Territory But Google Maps Is Getting Dangerously Close
excerpt: On models, reality, and the strange loop where Google Maps reshapes the world it claims to merely describe.

title: Imposter Syndrome Is Just Your Brain Running Antivirus on Your Confidence
excerpt: Why high performers doubt themselves, why incompetent people don't, and what AI hallucinations have to do with all of it.

title: Your Gut Feeling Is Just a Neural Network With No Documentation
excerpt: Intuition is pattern matching from compressed experience — a biological neural network that produces outputs without showing its work.

title: The Attention Economy Is a Ponzi Scheme (And We're All Invested)
excerpt: Attention is the new currency, but the supply is fixed and every app wants more. The maths doesn't work.

title: Why Your Smart Home Is Actually a Dumb Home With Good Marketing
excerpt: The reality of 'smart' home devices: connected, not intelligent. The gap between the brochure and your kitchen.

title: The Butterfly Effect But For Typos (How Tiny Mistakes Destroy Everything)
excerpt: How a missing hyphen destroyed a rocket, a wrong unit killed a Mars probe, and a software flag cost $440 million in 45 minutes.

title: Your Email Inbox Is a To-Do List Other People Write For You
excerpt: The fundamental problem with email: it gives everyone write access to your priority queue.

title: We Taught Sand to Think and Now It's Correcting Our Grammar
excerpt: The absurd journey from silicon to ChatGPT, told as the universe's most unlikely supply chain story.

title: Your Brain Has Two Employees and Neither of Them Have Read the Job Description
excerpt: On procrastination, AI alignment, and why your monkey brain is actually a misconfigured language model.

title: How Pokémon Go Players Accidentally Built the World's Best Robot Navigation System (And What That Says About Us)
excerpt: For ten years, millions of people walked around catching imaginary creatures. Turns out they were also training the most sophisticated pedestrian navigation dat

title: You Always Pick the Slowest Queue (And It's Not Bad Luck — It's Math)
excerpt: The universe isn't conspiring against you at the supermarket. But the math kind of is. Here's why your queue intuition is broken and what load balancers figured out decades ago.

title: Board Games Are Just Algorithms in a Trench Coat Pretending to Be Fun
excerpt: You spent Saturday night laughing, arguing, and grabbing plastic totems. Your brain spent Saturday night solving constraint satisfaction problems, running Bayesian inference, and hitting race conditions.
